77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC
22
72.
Robert F. Paget, 'The Great Antrum at Baiae', Vergilius 13 (1967)
42-50.
The epic claimed the lion's share of scholarship during the past year. Klingner's
text (25) is welcome and reliable. Quinn (26) treats Aeneid 1, 755-6 and 5, 626
and suggests that the wanderings of Aeneas occupied five years by our reckoning,
After the Greek departure in the spring and the city's collapse in the early summer,
the departure of the refugees came, in Quinn's view, one year later, at the start of
the following summer. Speranza's (27) annotated text is conservative and unimportant. Tracy (28) examines some of the ramifications of the Helenus episode.
Aeneid 4 has engaged the attention of several scholars with the major emphasis on
Dido, her predicament and her defense. The new availability of Pease's (31) marvelously pedantic and authoritative commentary is most welcome, and school and
college classrooms will also applaud Bertha Tilly's (35) serviceable and generally
attractive addition to the 'Palatine Classics'. However, students who are introduced
to Vergil through Bertha Tilly's text will hardly appreciate the impact of recent
and diverse scholarship on the best-loved book. Pearce (34) argues for the meaning
'kindly' in his interpretation of placidas viri deus obstruit auris (440). Koch's study
(36) emphasizes parallelisms in theme and imaginative language in the firstfour
books of the Aeneid. Galinsky (37) attacks the 'superfluous' approach to the Fifth
Aeneid and argues for its central role in the epic. He detects an Aeneid in parvo in
the maligned book, and believes that it was composed concomitantly with the rest
of the epic. The boxing-match between Dares and Entellus was, in his view, affected
by scenes in Aeneid 12, and is another indication that the book was finished late,
near the end of the Aeneid*s composition. Eichholz (40) detects a chronological
scheme in the composition of the shield's subject matter, scenes which were creditable to Rome and flattering to Roman pride. Dynamic rather than thematic structure
explains the rationale of the content. The Golden Bough causes Koch (41) to make
a botanical examination of 'classic' mistletoe, and Segal (42) to engage with a symbolical interpretation of the 'delaying' action of the talisman. Segal interprets the
hesitation as a symbolical anticipation of the rude loss of innocence which awaits
the peaceful forest of Italy - attacked so purposefully and sacreligiously by Agrippa
on the slopes of Avernus. Segal finds the bough's effort to retain its integrity as
comparable to the forced acquiescence of Saturnian Italy and Saturnian Juno to
the dictates of Jupiter and the security of the Trojan destiny. Maurach's study (44)
of Ascanius' archery and his role in Aeneid 9 explores Homeric and Hellenistic
analogies in determining the meaning and the proper function of Ascanius-Julus.
Fontenrose's polemic (45) casts much fresh light on the formalities and implications
of Vergilian oaths at critical junctures in the action. Raymond Bloch (46) examines
the implications of Servius adAen 5, 422 (an allusion to Etruscan triadic design in
temple architecture), the equation of Juno with Astarte (the Tanit of Carthage) in
Aeneid 1, 44 If., with sidelights on the Pyrgi gold plate inscriptions, and finally the
Thracian sacrifice {Aeneid 3, 18ff) and the awful manifestation of Polydorus' murder.
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC
23
Davis (47) charges that the hunt theme reveals that the epic was no simple paean to
Imperial Rome but 'Vergil's personal commentary on the costs of empire, paid by
victors and victims'. Dilke (48) detects some of the simpler numerical elements (3,
7, 12, and 30) in the poem and investigates whether they were deliberate insertions
or not. His article is a timely relief for those who favour structural analysis over
complicated mathematics. Duckworth (49) offers a detailed discussion of the structure of the epic, basic themes, and similarities and contrasts. Inter alia Duckworth
treats the Evander episode and its valuable effect on the latter part of the Aeneid,
and provides in passing some good remarks on Rome's topography and the shield
(78-80). Hornsby (50) explores the recurrent figure of the 'shepherd' in Vergil's
works, from the knowledgeable pastor of the Eclogues, to the 'shepherd of the
people' in the epic. Hornsby (51) also examines the recurrent pattern of the wearing
of spoils and its disastrous consequences throughout the epic. Each episode is examined in the context of its book with critical discussion of the suggestive nature of
the pattern which culminates in Turnus' death. McDevitt (53) concludes that
hysteron proteron does not exist in the epic as a linguistic trick or ornament; the
reversal of clauses is deliberately designed to provide a particular poetic effect.
Morland's article (54) on the influence of Horace's Odes on the Aeneid indicates
that further work remains to be done along comparable lines. Nethercut (55) notes
a latent tendency in the Trojans to furor and aggrandisement and so an identification
with the nature of the Greek invaders of Troy. Kenneth Quinn's (57) long awaited
study of the Aeneid is a masterly subjective critical description which deserves to
be on every reference shelf. It will excite much argument and probably adverse
comment for its fresh and contentious approach to hallowed areas of 'scholarship
sublime', but will be enlightening to most and a fine support to the growing appreciation of Roman literature in general. Rexroth's (58) pithy remarks on the Aeneid
assess the epic as an anti-heroic poem wherein the civil conscience argues against
heroic virtue in the shape of a counter-Odyssey. Outstretched hands provide a
common gesture in the Aeneid, and Sullivan's (59) systematic review of gestures
to gods and humans emphasizes the persuasive and expressive potency of the
gesture. Williams (61) uncovers a private and public voice in Vergil, the former
intense, sympathetic and pitying, the other concerned with pacific behavior and
the civilizing mission of Roman imperialism. But the divorce from real life, the
mythological aspects of the epic, inspired by literature, art, dreams and Homer,
constitutes the most genuine expression of the poet. The role of the gods has been
freshly examined by several scholars usually with the conclusion that the Italian
conceptions of divinity are the most appealing and affecting to the poet. Wlosok's
study (66) is a systematic detailed assessment of Aeneid 1, 223-304, 305-417, and
Aeneid 8, 608-728, with 520-40. Castagnoli's archaeological quests into Aeneas'
Latium continue to yield valuable illustration and confirmation of the Vergilian
allusions. Both articles (67, 68) deal with the sanctuary zone at Lavinium, the 13
altars, consecrated perhaps to the Penates, the problems connected with Troia, the
Lucus Solis Indigetis, and the river Numicus. Schweizer (70) casts valuable light on
Italic elements in the enic and the noet's overriding concern and svmoathv for the
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC
24
Italian components of the united peninsula. Pageťs (71, 72) explorations at Baiae
and in the environs of Cumae give every promise of awakening new interest in
Campanian archaeology particularly in the critical areas of Cumae's suzerainty and
influence. His discovery of a complex of tunnels at Baiae, equated with an Oracle
of the Dead, perhaps prematurely, has caused Vergilian scholars to review the
descensus Averno with new curiosity. The tunnels and the terminal chamber, the
6th century Greek temple at the point of entry, and the careful provision for light
and easy access along the lengthy descent, suggest that the tunnels were designed
for some ritual purpose, perhaps initiation, long before Rome's advent. Agrippa's
depradations at Cumae and Avernus may have extended to Baiae as well for the
complex was evidently blocked during the period 50-25 B.C. either as a consequence
of volcanic action or because of discrimination against a venerable cult.
APPENDIX VERGILIANA, VITAE, ETC.
73.

Some Papers Presented to the Classics Sections of the Twentieth
University ofKentucky Foreign Languages Conference, Lexington,
Kentucky, 1967.

(1966) 77-80.

(1966) 67-113, especially 86-101.
78.
P.T. Eden, 'Adnotatiunculae in Appendicem Vergilianam', Mnemos.,
20 (1967) 285-92.
The Appendix continues to excite scholarly emendations and resolute comment.
Duckworth's study yields fresh encouragement to the continuation of research and
study in the Appendix. His lengthy article (77) argues that the Culex and Moretum
are probably youthful works, that the Ciris and Dirae, one poem not two, are not
Vergilian, and that the Aetna was probably written ca. 25-50 A.D.
79.
J.Bayet, 'L'expéíience sociale de Virgile', Mélanges de littérature
latine, (Paris, 1966) 281-302.

Ferdinando Castagnoli, 'I luoghi connessi con l'arrivo di Enea nel Lazio,Archaeologia Classica, 19 (1967) 235-47. 68. Robert Hale, London, 1967. 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC 22 72. Robert F. Paget, 'The Great Antrum at Baiae', Vergilius 13 (1967) 42-50. The epic claimed the lion's share of scholarship during the past year. Klingner's text (25) is welcome and reliable. Quinn (26) treats Aeneid 1, 755-6 and 5, 626 and suggests that the wanderings of Aeneas occupied five years by our reckoning, After the Greek departure in the spring and the city's collapse in the early summer, the departure of the refugees came, in Quinn's view, one year later, at the start of the following summer. Speranza's (27) annotated text is conservative and unimportant. Tracy (28) examines some of the ramifications of the Helenus episode. Aeneid 4 has engaged the attention of several scholars with the major emphasis on Dido, her predicament and her defense. The new availability of Pease's (31) marvelously pedantic and authoritative commentary is most welcome, and school and college classrooms will also applaud Bertha Tilly's (35) serviceable and generally attractive addition to the 'Palatine Classics'. However, students who are introduced to Vergil through Bertha Tilly's text will hardly appreciate the impact of recent and diverse scholarship on the best-loved book. Pearce (34) argues for the meaning 'kindly' in his interpretation of placidas viri deus obstruit auris (440). Koch's study (36) emphasizes parallelisms in theme and imaginative language in the firstfour books of the Aeneid. Galinsky (37) attacks the 'superfluous' approach to the Fifth Aeneid and argues for its central role in the epic. He detects an Aeneid in parvo in the maligned book, and believes that it was composed concomitantly with the rest of the epic. The boxing-match between Dares and Entellus was, in his view, affected by scenes in Aeneid 12, and is another indication that the book was finished late, near the end of the Aeneid*s composition. Eichholz (40) detects a chronological scheme in the composition of the shield's subject matter, scenes which were creditable to Rome and flattering to Roman pride. Dynamic rather than thematic structure explains the rationale of the content. The Golden Bough causes Koch (41) to make a botanical examination of 'classic' mistletoe, and Segal (42) to engage with a symbolical interpretation of the 'delaying' action of the talisman. Segal interprets the hesitation as a symbolical anticipation of the rude loss of innocence which awaits the peaceful forest of Italy - attacked so purposefully and sacreligiously by Agrippa on the slopes of Avernus. Segal finds the bough's effort to retain its integrity as comparable to the forced acquiescence of Saturnian Italy and Saturnian Juno to the dictates of Jupiter and the security of the Trojan destiny. Maurach's study (44) of Ascanius' archery and his role in Aeneid 9 explores Homeric and Hellenistic analogies in determining the meaning and the proper function of Ascanius-Julus. Fontenrose's polemic (45) casts much fresh light on the formalities and implications of Vergilian oaths at critical junctures in the action. Raymond Bloch (46) examines the implications of Servius adAen 5, 422 (an allusion to Etruscan triadic design in temple architecture), the equation of Juno with Astarte (the Tanit of Carthage) in Aeneid 1, 44 If., with sidelights on the Pyrgi gold plate inscriptions, and finally the Thracian sacrifice {Aeneid 3, 18ff) and the awful manifestation of Polydorus' murder. 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC 23 Davis (47) charges that the hunt theme reveals that the epic was no simple paean to Imperial Rome but 'Vergil's personal commentary on the costs of empire, paid by victors and victims'. Dilke (48) detects some of the simpler numerical elements (3, 7, 12, and 30) in the poem and investigates whether they were deliberate insertions or not. His article is a timely relief for those who favour structural analysis over complicated mathematics. Duckworth (49) offers a detailed discussion of the structure of the epic, basic themes, and similarities and contrasts. Inter alia Duckworth treats the Evander episode and its valuable effect on the latter part of the Aeneid, and provides in passing some good remarks on Rome's topography and the shield (78-80). Hornsby (50) explores the recurrent figure of the 'shepherd' in Vergil's works, from the knowledgeable pastor of the Eclogues, to the 'shepherd of the people' in the epic. Hornsby (51) also examines the recurrent pattern of the wearing of spoils and its disastrous consequences throughout the epic. Each episode is examined in the context of its book with critical discussion of the suggestive nature of the pattern which culminates in Turnus' death. McDevitt (53) concludes that hysteron proteron does not exist in the epic as a linguistic trick or ornament; the reversal of clauses is deliberately designed to provide a particular poetic effect. Morland's article (54) on the influence of Horace's Odes on the Aeneid indicates that further work remains to be done along comparable lines. Nethercut (55) notes a latent tendency in the Trojans to furor and aggrandisement and so an identification with the nature of the Greek invaders of Troy. Kenneth Quinn's (57) long awaited study of the Aeneid is a masterly subjective critical description which deserves to be on every reference shelf. It will excite much argument and probably adverse comment for its fresh and contentious approach to hallowed areas of 'scholarship sublime', but will be enlightening to most and a fine support to the growing appreciation of Roman literature in general. Rexroth's (58) pithy remarks on the Aeneid assess the epic as an anti-heroic poem wherein the civil conscience argues against heroic virtue in the shape of a counter-Odyssey. Outstretched hands provide a common gesture in the Aeneid, and Sullivan's (59) systematic review of gestures to gods and humans emphasizes the persuasive and expressive potency of the gesture. Williams (61) uncovers a private and public voice in Vergil, the former intense, sympathetic and pitying, the other concerned with pacific behavior and the civilizing mission of Roman imperialism. But the divorce from real life, the mythological aspects of the epic, inspired by literature, art, dreams and Homer, constitutes the most genuine expression of the poet. The role of the gods has been freshly examined by several scholars usually with the conclusion that the Italian conceptions of divinity are the most appealing and affecting to the poet. Wlosok's study (66) is a systematic detailed assessment of Aeneid 1, 223-304, 305-417, and Aeneid 8, 608-728, with 520-40. Castagnoli's archaeological quests into Aeneas' Latium continue to yield valuable illustration and confirmation of the Vergilian allusions. Both articles (67, 68) deal with the sanctuary zone at Lavinium, the 13 altars, consecrated perhaps to the Penates, the problems connected with Troia, the Lucus Solis Indigetis, and the river Numicus. Schweizer (70) casts valuable light on Italic elements in the enic and the noet's overriding concern and svmoathv for the 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:19:45 UTC 24 Italian components of the united peninsula. Pageťs (71, 72) explorations at Baiae and in the environs of Cumae give every promise of awakening new interest in Campanian archaeology particularly in the critical areas of Cumae's suzerainty and influence. His discovery of a complex of tunnels at Baiae, equated with an Oracle of the Dead, perhaps prematurely, has caused Vergilian scholars to review the descensus Averno with new curiosity. The tunnels and the terminal chamber, the 6th century Greek temple at the point of entry, and the careful provision for light and easy access along the lengthy descent, suggest that the tunnels were designed for some ritual purpose, perhaps initiation, long before Rome's advent. Agrippa's depradations at Cumae and Avernus may have extended to Baiae as well for the complex was evidently blocked during the period 50-25 B.C. either as a consequence of volcanic action or because of discrimination against a venerable cult. His lengthy article (77) argues that the Culex and Moretum are probably youthful works, that the Ciris and Dirae, one poem not two, are not Vergilian, and that the Aetna was probably written ca. 25-50 A.D. 79. raw_text:
R.D. Williams, 'Servius - Commentator and Guide', PVS 6 (1966-7)
50-56.
Benario (80) has examined the affinity of Tacitus for Vergil, regarding the historian
as bard of Vergil's dream perverted, and Miss Bews (81) has concentrated at greater
length on the nature and significance of Vergilian influence on the language and
themes of the Annals. Brisson (82) is concerned with Vergil's life and offers specific
dates which will appeal to some, not to others:
e.g., 53-50 B.C., Vergil at Rome
attending the school of Epidius; 49 B.C., at Naples, with Siro; October, 45 B.C.,
return to Mantua; 43-41 B.C., composed Eclogues
2,3,5,7, at Mantua; 38 B.C.,
Vergil established in Campania (Geòrgie 1); 37 B.C., Eclogue 10 and publication of
the bucolic collection. Vergil's literary influence on authors other than Tacitus has
attracted several scholars, always with profit to the strait-jacketed Latinist (83, 85,
90, 91, 95, 97, 101, 108). D'Avanzo (85) argues that Keats' first descent of Endymion (Book 2) owes largely to Aeneid 6; Endymion's model is Aeneas and his
descent follows Aeneas' pattern very closely. Hudson-Williams (90) finds Vergilian
influence in the poetry of the late Latin period, the 4th and 5th centuries particularly. Lucan's indebtedness to Vergil is carefully reviewed by Thompson and Bruere
(108) and more imaginatively though incompletely by Morford (97). Juvenal's debt
to Vergil has been partially explored by Joly (95) but much remains to be done.
The reprint of the Platnauer Fifty Years of Classical Scholarship is disappointing
in a number of respects, not least in the failure to up-date Catullus and The Augustan Poets. Jocelyn's (92) study of Ennius' tragedies involves Vergil's use of Alexander and Medea. The reprint of E.K. Rand's Magical Art of Virgil (102) is enterprising and gratifying. Two collections of importance to the overall study of Vergil
are Swanson's word list (106) and Thompson's bibliography of American Ph.D.
dissertations (107). Schoder's (104) assessment of the portraiture of Vergil is perceptive but incomplete since it lacks Gisela Richter's recently identified versions
which are totally different from the Villa Medici relief type and the Bardo mosaic.
Williams, with customary insight, lauds Servius' commentary as a monumental work
of enormous erudition (on the whole) and solid common sense. Imaginative insights
and sensitive literary criticism are not ingredients of his encyclopedic antiquarian
commentary.
McMaster University
October 1, 1968.
A.G. McKay
